rationale: This is a canonical example of the Stobbe condensation, reacting a simple ketone (acetone) with a dialkyl succinate (diethyl succinate) in the presence of a strong base (sodium ethoxide). The product is the characteristic alkylidene succinic acid half-ester, formed after an acidic workup (implied). This example clearly illustrates the formation of a new carbon-carbon double bond and the hydrolysis of one of the two ester groups, which are the defining features of the Stobbe condensation.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, aliphatic substrates (acetone and diethyl succinate) to avoid overlap with other named reactions involving aromatic aldehydes/ketones (e.g., Perkin, Claisen-Schmidt) or other dicarbonyl compounds (e.g., Knoevenagel, Dieckmann). The unique product, an alkylidene succinic acid half-ester, is highly characteristic of the Stobbe condensation and distinct from the products of other common condensation reactions.
